coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible double emission boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection of Al3+ and Fe2+
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Cunjin
Sun, Qian
Li, Chenxi
Tang, Daibin
Shi, Huanxian
Liu, Enzhou
Guo, Pengqi
Xue, Weiming
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, dual-emission boron-nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (B,N-CQDs) were synthesized by a micro-solvothermal method, and the prepared B,N-CQDs were used to detect Al3+ and Fe2+. The doping of B elements changed the microstructure of CQDs and endowed them with a dual emission property (F430 and F500). The detection basis of Al3+ and Fe2+ with the B,N-CQDs were attributed to the reverse changes of double emission peaks( F430 and F500 ) and fluorescence intensity changes of B,N-CQDs, respectively. Besides, the limit of detection (LOD) of Al3+ and Fe2+as low as 0.187 μM and 0.276 μM, respectively. In addition, B,N-CQDs were loaded onto filter paper to prepare a paper-based sensor for the detection of Al3+ with the feature of being more portable and practical. Meanwhile, B,N-CQDs presented outstanding performance in the detection of Al3+ in tap water and Fe content in ferrous sulfate tablets.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Micro-solvothermal method
Boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots
Ratio fluorescent probe
Paper-based sensor
Detection Al3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ratio fluorescence probe based on B,N-CQDs to detect Al3+
Fluorescence probe based on B,N-CQDs to detect Fe2+
Detection of Al3+ and Fe2+ in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020M683701XB), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No. 2020JQ-609, 2018JM2037). The authors would also like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM and fluorescence lifetime analysis.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276 ), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020M683701XB ), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No. 2020JQ-609 , 2018JM2037 ). The authors would also like to thank Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the TEM and fluorescence lifetime analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (inset figure showed histogram of size distribution) and (b) HRTEM of N-CQDs; (c) TEM image (inset figure showed histogram of size distribution) and (d) HRTEM of B,N-CQDs.
XRD spectra (a), FTIR spectra (b, c), XPS survey (d), C1s XPS (e), N1s XPS (f), O1s XPS (g) and B1s XPS (h) of N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs.

(a, b) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs. (c, d) Ultraviolet absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs (the inset is a photo of CQDs solution under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right)). (e, f) The emission spectra of N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths.
(a) Three-dimensional fluorescence in the presence of BA in N-CQDs solution. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs and N-CQDs+BA. (c) CIE coordinates of N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs.

Fluorescence spectra (a), fluorescence intensity (b), and fluorescence photos under 365 nm UV lamp (c) in the presence of different metal ions with the concentration of 500 μM in B,N-CQDs solution. (d) The fluorescence spectra of B,N-CQDs solution in the presence of different concentrations of Al3+. (e) The plot between F430/F500 of B,N-CQDs and concentration of Al3+. (f) Photos of B, N-CQDs solutions with different concentrations of Al3+ under daylight (up) and 365 nm UV lamp (down).

Fluorescence spectra (a), fluorescence intensity (b), and fluorescence photos under365 nm UV lamp (c) in the presence of different metal ions in B,N-CQDs solution. (d) The fluorescence spectra of B,N-CQDs solution in the presence of different concentrations of Fe2+. (e) The plot between F/F0 of B,N-CQDs and concentration of Al3+. (f) Photos of B, N-CQDs solutions with different concentrations of Fe2+ under daylight (up) and 365 nm UV lamp (down).

Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of B,N-CQDs (a), B,N-CQDs+Al3+ (b) and B,N-CQDs+Fe2+ (c). UV absorption in the presence of different concentrations of Al3+ (d) or Fe2+ (e) in B,N-CQDs solution. (f) PL decay spectra of B,N-CQDs, B,N-CQDs+Al3+ and B,N-CQDs+Fe2+.
(a) The F430 decay spectra of B,N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs+Al3+. (b) The F500 decay spectra of B,N-CQDs, B,N-CQDs+Al3+ and B,N-CQDs+Fe2+.
Illustration of the synthesis of B,N-CQDs for detection Al3+ and Fe2+.
Comparison of proposed fluorescent probe with other methods for the detection of Al3+ and Fe2+.
Fluorescence lifetimes of F430 of B,N-CQDs in the presence and absence of Al3+.
Fluorescence lifetimes of F500 of B,N-CQDs in the presence and absence of Al3+ or Fe2+.
Recoveries of Al3+ in real samples.
Recoveries of Fe2+ in real samples.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible double emission boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots for selective and sensitive detection of Al3+ and Fe2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Chemical Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710069, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCollege of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest University, Xi'an 710069, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an 710127, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Physics, Northwest University, Xi'an 710127, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Shaanxi University of Chinese Medicine, Xianyang 712046, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dSchool of Pharmacy, Shaanxi University of Chinese Medicine, Xianyang, 712046, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, dual-emission boron-nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (B,N-CQDs) were synthesized by a micro-solvothermal method, and the prepared B,N-CQDs were used to detect Al3+ and Fe2+. The doping of B elements changed the microstructure of CQDs and endowed them with a dual emission property (F430 and F500). The detection basis of Al3+ and Fe2+ with the B,N-CQDs were attributed to the reverse changes of double emission peaks( F430 and F500 ) and fluorescence intensity changes of B,N-CQDs, respectively. Besides, the limit of detection (LOD) of Al3+ and Fe2+as low as 0.187 μM and 0.276 μM, respectively. In addition, B,N-CQDs were loaded onto filter paper to prepare a paper-based sensor for the detection of Al3+ with the feature of being more portable and practical. Meanwhile, B,N-CQDs presented outstanding performance in the detection of Al3+ in tap water and Fe content in ferrous sulfate tablets.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Boron nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: As the most abundant metallic element of the earth`s crust, aluminum is widely used in our daily life, such as food additives, aluminum cookware, pop-top can, toothpaste, stomach medicine that neutralizes stomach acid, etc. However, the excessive intake of Al3+ is harmful to the central nervous system and bones of the human body, especially can induce Alzheimer's disease and osteoporosis [1]. In addition, Al3+ in water is easily soluble in soil and absorbed by plants, which is a major factor limiting plant production [2]. Iron, as an essential element for the human body, plays a vital role in the living system. Fe3+ cannot be absorbed directly by the body for which needs to be converted into Fe2+ to be used by body. Deficiency of Fe2+ can cause anemia, which has become one of the most common nutritional diseases in the world [3]. Therefore, the development of a facile, sensitive and selective method for Al3+ and Fe2+ trace detection is crucial for monitoring the environment and maintaining human health. Up to now, the main methods for Al3+ and Fe2+ detection were inductively coupled plasma mass-spectrometry (ICP-MS), atomic absorption spectrometry, and colorimetry [4,5]. Despite providing accurate results, the above methods need complex operations and expensive instruments, which are costly and not conducive to widespread usage. The fluorescent probe is a powerful method for the detection of metal ions due to its advantages of simple operation, low cost, high sensitivity, and easy data analysis. The fluorescent materials involved in fluorescent probes include organic fluorescent dyes, metal-semiconductor quantum dots, noble metal nanoclusters, carbon-based fluorescent nanodots and so on [6,7]. Organic fluorescent dyes have poor photostability and water-solubility [8]. Metal-based fluorescence nanomaterials involve heavy metals and have poor biocompatibility and environmental protection [9]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a new “zero-dimensional” fluorescent nanomaterial in the carbon nanomaterials family, have been widely used in the detection of metal ions such as Ag+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Hg2+, Co2+ and Ni2+ due to their excellent optical stability, outstanding biocompatibility and low cost [10,11]. However, bifunctional sensors that can detect both Al3+ and Fe2+ are still rarely reported. Hence, the design and development of novel bidirectional sensors would be very interesting and significant due to the fact that its more versatile functions and have greater application prospects and advantages. Therefore, this work will contribute to the shortcomings in this regard.

Since the discovery of CQDs in 2004 [12], diversity of methods were used to prepare CQDs, such as hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave-assisted pyrolysis, template and ultrasonic method. etc [13–15]. Among them, the hydrothermal method and solvothermal method are very popular due to their simple operation process. However, according to reports, most of the CQDs prepared by the hydrothermal method emit shorter-wavelength fluorescence due to contain a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups [16]. The short-wavelength fluorescence is not conducive to biological application in vivo because of its weak penetration, high energy, and great damage to biological tissues. On the contrary, most CQDs with long emission wavelengths were prepared by the solvothermal method [17–19]. The reason is that in solvents with poorer hydrophilicity and lower polarity, oxygen-related groups are restricted, while nitrogen-containing fluorophores are easily formed, resulting in a decrease in short-wavelength emission and an increase in long-wavelength emission [20]. However, the risks of temperatures and pressures of solvothermal processes posing potential hazard for the personal safety and laboratory safety due to the involvement of organic solvents. Generally, two solutions could be adopted to avoid the potential danger of high pressure while maintaining the advantages of the solvent heat method at the same time. One of them is to reduce the reaction temperature, and the other is to use trace organic solvents. At low temperatures, the carbon source cannot be carbonized completely and the produced CQDs have low crystallinity and poor optical properties, so lowering the temperature seems not feasible. Because the pressure in the hydrothermal reactor at high temperature is proportional to the amount of solvent, reducing the amount of solvent can reduce the potential dangers caused by high pressure and maintain the advantages of the solvothermal method. Therefore, in order to prepare long-wavelength CQDs, a solvothermal method with trace amount of solvent (micro-solvothermal method) will be used in this work. Another scientific question is how to improve quantum yield (QY). Heteroatom doping is an attractive and effective strategy to enhance the QY of CQDs through altering the electronic structure of core and the surface states [21]. For example, the internal electronic environment of CQDs can be adjusted by supplying electrons from N to the C atom due to the fact that the N atom possesses high electronegativity [22]; The doping of CQDs with B atom can effectively regulate the electronic and QY of CQDs because the vacant p-orbital of B atom can effectively conjugate with C=C atoms by reducing the distance between B3+ and the sp2 clusters of CQDs, which could enhance the electronic delocalization in the carbon matrix and increase radiative recombination rate [23–25]. The co-doping of N and B atoms into CQDs may produce some surprising results. However, to the best of our knowledge, the reports about B,N-CQDs is still rare. Therefore, this work will prepare B,N-CQDs and explore the changes in microstructure and optical properties caused by co-doping.

Herein, the dual-emission B,N-CQDs for the detection of Al3+ and Fe2+ was synthesized successfully from L-dopa (L-DA) and boric acid (BA) by a new micro-solvothermal method (Scheme 1). B,N-CQDs exhibited yellow-green fluorescence with higher fluorescence QY and larger size compared to blue fluorescent N-CQDs. In the presence of Fe2+, the fluorescence intensity of B,N-CQDs was dramatically quenched owing to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and electron transfer effect (ET). However, in the presence of Al3+, the fluorescence color of B,N-CQDs gradually changed from yellow-green to blue due to the aggregation-induced emission enhancement effect (AIEE). Hence, a fluorescence sensor based on colorimetric was created for visual detection of Al3+. Besides, the paper-based sensor was prepared for the detection of Al3+ based on the change of fluorescence color, which has potential application prospects. Meanwhile, B,N-CQDs present excellent biocompatibility and exhibit excellent detection performance in the detection of Al3+ in tap water and iron content analysis in ferrous sulfate tablets, respectively.

L-dopa (L-DA) was acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Boric acid (BA) was purchased from Tianjin jindongtianzheng precision chemical reagent factory. Citric acid monohydrate (CA) was acquired from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Quinine sulfate dihydrate (AR) was acquired from PAI NI Chemical Reagent Factory (Zhengzhou, China). Sulfuric acid was got from Xi'an Sanpu Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Aluminum nitrate nonahydrate and ferrous sulfate were obtained from Tianjin Hengxing Chemical Reagent Manufacturing Co., Ltd. Phosphate-buffered solution (pH 7.2-7.4) was purchased from Quanzhou Hongke Biotechnology Co., Ltd. HeLa cell line and macrophages (RAW 264.7) was provided by Shaanxi University of Traditional Chinese Medicine. The 3-(4,5)-dimethylthiahiazo (-z-y1)-3,5-di- phenytetrazoliumromide (MTT) was obtained from Solarbio Life Sciences. Dimethyl sulfoxide was purchased from Tianjin kemeo Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Reagents for interference detection were placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information section. All the reagents were analytical grade and had not undergone further purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiment process.
The instrument information of work was placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

As shown in Scheme 1, B,N-CQDs were prepared via a micro-solvothermal method using L-DA and BA as C, N and B sources respectively. Briefly, 0.2 g of L-DA and 0.1 g of BA were dissolved in 2 mL ethanol. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was then heated at 150 °C for 6 h and cooled naturally to room temperature. The obtained product was dissolved in 100 mL of water. Subsequently, the mixture solution was centrifuged for 10 min at 10 000 rpm and the obtained supernatant was dialyzed for 48 h using a dialysis bag (1000 Da). The supernatant was collected and dried at 60 °C oven to obtain a light brown solid powder.
To consider the effect of the B/N double doping strategy, N-CQDs were prepared in accordance with the described above method without adding BA.

The detection of Al3+ was carried out in ultra-pure water at room temperature. In a typical assay, a series of Al3+ aqueous solutions with various concentrations were freshly prepared previously and added into the B,N-CQDs aqueous solution (0.01 mg mL−1). Then, after 10 min of incubation at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was recorded at the wavelength of 430 nm and 500 nm under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm.

In order to avoid the influence of other metal ions, the experiment of detecting Fe2+ was carried out in B,N-CQDs aqueous solution with 50 μM CA with strong metal ion complexing ability. Except that B, N-CQDs solution contains CA, the other details of detection Fe2+ are the same as the detection of Al3+.
The Cytotoxicity study of B,N-CQDs of work was placed in the Electronic Supplementary Information.

To obtain B,N-CQDs with better optical properties, the solvent volume, ratio of C/N source to B source, reaction temperature, and reaction time were optimized. As shown in Fig. S1, B,N-CQDs had the best double emission characteristics at 430 nm and 500 nm when the amount of ethanol was 2 mL, mass ratio of L-DA to BA was 1:0.5, reaction temperature was 150 °C and reaction time was 6 h. The B,N-CQDs prepared under optimal reaction condition was used for characterization and application.

The morphology and particle size distribution of N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs were investigated through TEM and HRTEM, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1 a, N-CQDs exhibited well-dispersion without any evident aggregation, the shape of them is quasi-spherical like with sizes of 1.83−4.13 nm, and the average particle size of 2.83 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) showed that N-CQD had distinct lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, corresponding to (100) crystal plane in the graphite phase [26]. Compared with N-CQDs, B,N-CQDs exhibited different particle sizes and lattice stripes. As shown in Fig. 1c, the particle size of B,N-CQDs was distributed between 8.10 and 15.54 nm with an average particle size of 11.81 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1d) showed that B,N-CQDs had two lattice fringes of 0.21 nm and 0.24 nm, respectively, corresponding to the (100) crystal planes of the graphite phase [27,28]. The appearance of the 0.21 nm lattice spacing in B,N-CQDs may be attributed to the reduction of the interplanar spacing of carbon due to B doping [29]. The crystal structure information of CQDs was further investigated by XRD.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, the XRD of B,N-CQDs had obvious diffraction peaks at 14.52°, 22° and 27.93°, respectively. The diffraction peak at 22° was attributed to the (002) peak plane of graphitic carbon [23], which was slightly red-shifted relative to the diffraction peak of N-CQDs due to the reduction of the interplanar spacing of carbon by B doping. This is consistent with the results of HRTEM. The diffraction peaks at 14.52 and 27.93 were attributed to the (010) and (002) peak surface of BA, respectively, blue-shifted relative to the diffraction peak of BA, which mainly due to the fact that B increased the interplanar spacing of carbon while decreased its own interplanar spacing [29]. The FTIR spectroscopy was used to investigate the characteristic groups of B,N-CQDs in Fig. 2b and Fig. 2c. It can be found that B, N-CQDs not only had functional groups similar to N-CQDs such as -NH2/-OH (3196 cm−1), C=C (1606 cm−1) and C-O (1051 cm−1) but also had unique B-N/B-O (1400 cm−1) functional groups and a higher content of C-O bonds [29,30]. In the FTIR data of B, N-CQDs, the B-C bond was not found [30,31]. It can be inferred that B atoms did not form a bond with carbon atoms, but existed in CQDs in the form of B-O and B-N, which will be further verified by XPS spectroscopy.

The elemental composition and valence state of N-CQDs, B,N-CQDs were identified by XPS analysis. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2d, Table S1) revealed four characteristic peaks at 191.6, 284.8, 400.71, and 532.4 eV, corresponding to the binding energy of B 1s, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The O to C ratios in N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs were 0.292 and 0.378, respectively, and the higher ratio of oxygen in B,N-CQDs may originate from the oxidizing properties of BA. In addition, high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s of B,N-CQDs in Fig. 2e could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.8, 286.1 and 289.15 eV, corresponding to C=C/C-C, C-N/C-O and C=N/C=O, respectively [32]. The N 1s XPS of B,N-CQDs in Fig. 2f exhibited three components at 400.05, 401.75 and 406.7 eV, which were identified as C-N=C, -NH2 and -NO2 [33]. The high resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 2g) was fitted for the three peaks at 531.6, 532.15 and 533.35 eV, which corresponded to C=O, C-O, and O-C=O, respectively [34]. The B1s XPS (Fig. 2h) was convolved into two peaks at 192.35 and 193.55 eV, attributed to B-N and B-O, respectively [34–36]. The XPS bonding information of B,N-CQDs and N-CQDs were shown in Table S2 and Table S3, and in combination with Fig. 2, two findings can be drawn. Firstly, C=N/C=O (289.15 eV), C-N=C (400.05 eV), -NH2 (401.75 eV) and -NO2 (406.7 eV) in B,N-CQDs have higher binding energy compared to N -CQDs; Secondly, B,N-CQDs have higher contents of C-N/C-O (286.1 eV), -NO2 (406.7 eV), C=O (531.6 eV) and O-C=O (533.35 eV) compared to those of N-CQDs. Both of these phenomena are caused by the increased oxidation of CQDs due to the oxidizing BA. In addition, considering that there is no B-C bond information in XPS, combined with the FT-IR spectrum, it can be seen that B is not bound to the skeleton of C, but only to N and O in CQDs. It is also possible that there is too little B-C to detect its signal.

Optical properties of N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs were investigated using three-dimensional fluorescence, UV-Vis absorption and two-dimensional fluorescence spectroscopy, respectively (Fig. 3 ). The fluorescence center in the three-dimensional (3 D) fluorescence spectrum was expressed as (EX, EM). As shown in Fig. 3a and 3b, the fluorescence center of N-CQDs was located at (350, 430), the fluorescence center of B,N-CQDs was located at (340, 500), while the two-dimensional spectrum showed that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of N-CQDs (Fig. 3c) were 350 nm and 430 nm, respectively. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of B,N-CQDs (Fig. 3d) were 340 nm and 500 nm, respectively. Additionally, the emission peak of B,N-CQDs had a double emission feature with a small shoulder peak at 430 nm. The aqueous solution of N-CQDs had a light inky color under sunlight and a strong blue fluorescence under UV lamp, while the aqueous solution of B,N-CQDs had a light yellow color under sunlight and a strong yellow-green fluorescence under UV lamp. The absorption spectrum of N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs exhibited strong absorption around 280 nm and 340 nm, which could be ascribed to π-π* transition of C=C bonds and n-π* transitions of C=O/C=N [10]. The fluorescence emission of CQDs was measured at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3e and 3f, N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs showed different degrees of excitation-dependence, which may arise from different surface state emissions and different particle sizes [37].

To investigate the contribution of BA to the fluorescence emission in B,N-CQDs, the following experiments were conducted. Briefly, 0.01 g of BA was added to 4 mL of N-CQDs solution, and the 3D fluorescence (Fig. 4 a) and 2D fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4b) were measured after 1 min. After oxidation of N-CQDs by BA, the fluorescence emission at 430 nm was reduced, while the emission at 500 nm was enhanced, which showed almost the same fluorescence emission behavior as that of B,N-CQDs. Combining the results of FTIR and XPS, it can be viewed that BA mainly played an oxidizing role in the preparation of B,N-CQDs. Additionally, Commission Internationale de l'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity diagram exhibited emission color of N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs in Fig. 4c, indicating that emission wavelengths were red-shifted with the doping of B. CIE coordinate of max emission intensity was located at (0.16, 0.12) and (0.19, 0.37), respectively, confirming the obvious blue emission of N-CQD and cyan-blue emission of B,N-CQDs. In the results of CIE coordinates, the fluorescence color of N-CQDs is consistent with that at 365 nm UV lamp, while the fluorescence color of B,N-CQDs is not. It may be caused by the excitation dependence of CQDs. The QY of N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs were calculated by the five-point method (Fig. S2) as 14.5% and 20.2%, respectively. The higher QY of B,N-CQDs compared to N-CQDs may be due to the B doping resulting in a double-emission channel that allows more electrons to return to the valence band by radiative leap [38].

B,N-CQDs have promising application prospects compared to N-CQDs due to longer fluorescence emission wavelengths and dual emission characteristics. The stability of B,N-CQDs was evaluated by recording the influences of ionic strength, photobleaching resistance, and pH on the emission intensity at 430 nm (F430) and 500 nm (F500), respectively. No obvious changes were observed after high ionic strength (0-1 M) and irradiation with 365 nm ultraviolet light (5 h) as illustrated in Fig. S3a and Fig. S3b. As shown in Fig. S3c and Fig. S3d, F500 and F430 showed different responses at different pH (1-14). F500 had a stable intensity value at pH 1-6, while it gradually decreased when pH was greater than 6. F430 kept a stable fluorescence intensity in a neutral environment (pH 7-9), while the intensity gradually decreased in acidic and alkaline environments. The pH sensitivity of B,N-CQDs may be caused by the protonation and deprotonation of functional groups on the surface of B,N-CQDs [39], and stronger evidence needed to be further investigated in future work. The initial pH of the prepared B,N-CQDs solution was 7, so the detection of the Al3+ sensor's optimal pH was set at pH 7 for further fluorescence analysis. Under this condition, the excellent stability of B,N-CQDs can fully meet the requirements of actual sample detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratio fluorescence probe based on B,N-CQDs to detect Al3+
ce:section.ce:para:
Selectivity and sensitivity were the two most important indicators to evaluate the performance of a fluorescent probe. The emission spectra of B,N-CQDs with 500 μM different metal ions including Ag+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Hg2+ and Ni4+ were depicted in Fig. 5 . Among those metal ions, although Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+ and Al3+ all caused changes in the fluorescence spectrum of B,N-CQDs (Fig. 5a), only Al3+ caused a significant increase in the ratio of B,N-CQDs dual emission peaks (F430/F500, Fig. 5b), because Al3+ quenched F500 and enhanced F430. When other metal ions coexist with Al3+, it does not affect this result. Therefore, it can be proved that the ratio fluorescent probe based on B, N-CQDs had excellent selectivity to Al3+. Moreover, it can be observed clearly with the naked eyes that only Al3+ caused a significant change in the fluorescent color of the B,N-CQDs solution, from yellow-green to blue (Fig. 5c). Fig. 5d offered the fluorescence emission spectra of B,N-CQDs with various Al3+ concentrations. With Al3+ concentration enhancement, F500 of B,N-CQDs gradually reduced, and F430 gradually increased. Furthermore, Fig. 5e demonstrated that the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F430/F500 and Al3+ concentrations in the range from 0-20 μM obeyed the following equations:
(1)
F 430
/
F 500
= 0.244 + 0.087 C
[
A
l
3 +
]

The correlation coefficient (R2) is 0.991, and the LOD for the Al3+ assay is 0.187 μM calculated following the 3σ criteria. Furthermore, Fig. 5f provided the photos of B,N-CQDs solutions incubated with various Al3+ concentrations under daylight and UV lamp. With the increase of Al3+ concentration, the B,N-CQDs solutions had no significant changes under daylight, and under UV lamp, the B,N-CQDs solutions changed from yellow-green fluorescence to blue fluorescence. The change in fluorescence color of the solution showed the potential to visualize the detection of Al3+. The visual sensor carrier generally included liquid sensors and paper-based sensors. Compared to liquid sensors, paper-based sensors have the advantage of being portable and field testable. Therefore, in this work, the paper-based sensor based on B,N-CQDs was obtained by soaking the filter paper in the B,N-CQDs solution for 0.5 h and then taking it out and drying it in an oven at 60 °C. As shown in Fig. S4a, the prepared paper-based sensor exhibited yellow-green fluorescence under UV Lamp, and when 100 μL of different metal ions were added dropwise it was found that only Al3+ caused a significant change in fluorescence from yellow-green to blue in the paper-based sensor. Furthermore, the fluorescence changes of the paper-based sensor after adding different concentrations of Al3+ were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4b, the fluorescence of the filter paper gradually turned blue with the increase of Al3+ concentration from 0 to 20 μM. This result proved that the prepared paper-based sensor can semi-quantitatively detect aluminum ions and had potential practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence probe based on B,N-CQDs to detect Fe2+
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 5a, besides Fe2+ can quench the fluorescence of B,N-CQDs, Cu2+ and Fe3+ can also quench the fluorescence of B,N-CQDs. To eliminate the effect of Cu2+ and Fe3+, the detection of Fe2+ can be performed in CA solution with strong metal complexation ability. Because the CA solution is weakly acidic, and B,N-CQDs only have an emission peak at 500 nm in an acidic environment, only F500 needs to be recorded for the detection of Fe2+. As shown in Fig. 6 a, among those metal ions including Ag+, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Hg2+ and Ni4+, only Fe2+ significantly quenched the fluorescence signal of B,N-CQDs in CA solution. The fluorescence signals (F/F0) of B,N-CQDs in CA solution co-existing with one or two metal ions were shown in Fig. 6b, and the results indicated that B,N-CQDs had good specificity and selectivity for Fe2+. The photos of the B,N-CQDs solution containing different metal ions under UV lamp were shown in Fig. 6c, and the fluorescence of the B,N-CQDs solution containing Fe2+ was completely quenched.

Fig. 6d showed the fluorescence emission spectra of B,N-CQDs with various Fe2+ concentrations. With Fe2+ concentration enhancement, F500 of B,N-CQDs gradually reduced. Furthermore, Fig. 6e demonstrated that the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F0 and Fe2+ concentrations in the range from 0 to 10 μM and 10−25 μM obeyed the Eqs. (2) and (3), respectively:
(2)
F /
F 0
= 0.960 − 0.059 C
[
F
e
2 +
]
,
R
2
= 0.975 ,
0 − 10
μ M
(3)
F /
F 0
= 0.484 − 0.010 C
[
F
e
2 +
]
,
R
2
= 0.950 ,
10 − 25
μ M

The limit of detection for Fe2+ assay is 0.276 μM calculated following the 3σ criteria. Furthermore, Fig. 6f provided the photos of B,N-CQDs solutions incubated with various Fe2+ concentrations under daylight and UV lamp. With the increase of Fe2+ concentration, the B,N-CQDs solution gradually turned light black under sunlight may be due to the agglomeration of B,N-CQDs. Under the UV lamp, the yellow-green fluorescence of B,N-CQDs solution was gradually quenched with the increase of Fe2+ concentration.

The performances of the B,N-CQD-based Al3+ and Fe2+ fluorescent probes are summarized in Table 1 . Compared with the previous fluorescent probes for Al3+ and Fe2+, the B,N-CQDs fluorescent probe established in this work exhibited a comparable detection range and relatively low LOD.
In order to investigate the detection mechanism, the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra and UV absorption spectra of B,N-CQDs were measured before and after the addition of detection objects.

For the mechanism of Al3+ detection, as shown in Fig. 7 a, and Fig. 7b, the fluorescence emission center of B,N-CQDs shifted from (340, 500) to (340, 430) after the addition of Al3+, and the shift of the fluorescence center proved that a new substance may be generated. Generally speaking, the generation of new substances means a new UV absorption peak could appear. However, we just can see that UV absorption peaks at 340 nm of the mixed solution of B,N-CQDs and Al3+ was enhanced and no new absorption peak appeared with the increase of Al3+ concentration (Fig. 7d). Therefore, it can be preliminarily judged that the movement of the fluorescence center is not caused by the formation of new substances. It may be due to the change in the ratio of surface state emission to nuclear state emission of B,N-CQDs caused by Al3+ through enhanced surface state emission or nuclear state emission. Therefore, the detection mechanism was further studied by investigating the change of fluorescence lifetime at F430 and F500. As shown in Fig. 8 a and Table 2 , the fluorescence lifetimes at F430 are 10.115 ns for B,N-CQDs, and 4.900 ns for B,N-CQDs+Al3+. The τ1 in the fluorescence lifetime generally refers to the nuclear state emission and τ2 refers to the surface state emission [48]. It can be seen from Table 2 that the percentage of surface state emission decreased (from 53.76% to 11.67%) and the percentage of nuclear state emission increased (from 42.64% to 88.33%) for B,N-CQDs+Al3+. For F500 (Fig. 8b and Table 3 ), the fluorescence lifetimes of B,N-CQDs and B,N-CQDs+Al3+ were 9.948 ns and 11.221 ns, respectively. The percentage of surface state emission (τ2) increased (from 72.19% to 78.61%) and the percentage of nuclear state emission (τ1) decreased (from 27.81% to 21.39%) for B,N-CQDs+Al3+. Compared to F500, the degree of fluorescence is more variable in F430 and the nuclear state emission dominates. The above data can prove that Al3+ causes the enhanced emission from the nuclear state of B,N-CQDs, and also can laterally prove that the emission at 430 nm of B,N-CQDs was attributed to the nuclear state emission. The TEM data of the mixed solution in the presence of Al3+ were further tested, as shown in Fig. S5a, where the particle size of B,N-CQDs increased to 28.0 nm. The larger the particle size, the smaller the specific surface area. For the luminescence mechanism of CQDs, the percentage of surface state emission will decrease and the percentage of nuclear state emission will increase as the particle size increases. Combining with the fluorescence lifetime data results, it can be concluded that the detection mechanism of Al3+-induced F430 enhancement belongs to the aggregation-induced enhancement emission (AIEE) effect [49,50].

For the Fe2+ detection mechanism, as shown in Fig. 7a and Fig. 7c, Fe2+ reduced the fluorescence intensity of B,N-CQDs, but did not change the luminescence center of B,N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 7e, the addition of Fe2+ darkened the color of the B,N-CQDs solution and increased the UV absorption baseline, proving that larger size particles were generated. Therefore, the TEM of B,N-CQDs+Fe2+ was measured, and as shown in Fig. S5b, the mixed solution underwent significant agglomeration and the average size increased to 33.03 nm. The above results can prove that the fluorescence quenching caused by Fe2+ belonged to ACQ. The ACQ belongs to static quenching, that is, the fluorescence lifetime will not change significantly [51]. However, as shown in Fig. 8f and Table 3, the presence of Fe2+ decreased the fluorescence lifetime of the B,N-CQDs solution (from 9.948 ns to 6.121 ns), which proves that there is an electron transfer contribution to the decrease of fluorescence in this mixed solution [10]. The reason is that Fe2+ has a certain oxidation capacity and can induce the transfer of excited state electrons of B,N-CQDs to it. Therefore, the detection mechanism of Fe2+ may be the synergistic effect of agglomeration-induced quenching and electron transfer (ET).

For the detection of real samples, the toxicity of fluorescent probes is a very important factor. Therefore, the cytotoxicity of B,N-CQDs was assessed by MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S6, the results of the MTT assay showed that the cell viability remained above 85% after the addition of BN-CQDs (up to 100 μg/mL) in cancer cell lines (Hela) and normal cells (RAW 264.7), indicating that B, N-CQDs were almost non-cytotoxic to cancer cells and normal cells. The results showed that B,N-CQDs had good biosafety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Al3+ and Fe2+ in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability of B, N-CQDs fluorescent probe in actual sample analysis, the content of Al3+ in tap water and the content of Fe2+ in ferrous sulfate tablets were detected, respectively. The results of detection of Al3+ in tap water obtained by the standard addition methods were shown in Table 4 . The satisfactory recoveries (99.781%-106.342%) indicated that B,N-CQDs fluorescent probe had potential application in the real sample. As shown in Table 5 , the content of the effective Fe in ferrous sulfate tablets was detected to be 63.7 mg with this method, the standard content was 60 mg, and the relative error was 6.17%. The result proved that the fluorescent probe based on B,N-CQDs could rapidly and accurately determine the concentration of Fe2+ in practical samples.

In summary, a safe and easy-to-operate micro-solvothermal method has been used to synthesize yellow-green emission multifunctional B,N-CQDs. Due to B doping, the B,N-CQDs showed a high quantum yield of 20.2%, large particle size of 11.81 nm and clear lattice fringes of 0.21 nm and 0.24 nm. In terms of application, the B,N-CQDs fluorescent probes have outstanding characteristics of high sensitivity, rapid response time, wide response range, and excellent biocompatibility, which makes them utilized for detection of Al3+ and Fe2+, with a wide detection range of 0−20 μM and 0−25 μM, and low limit of detection of 0.187 μM and 0.276 μM, respectively. Furthermore, B,N-CQDs have been prepared as paper-based sensors for the detection of Al3+ with more portable and practical features. In addition, B,N-CQD is used to quantify Al3+ in actual tap water and Fe2+ in ferrous sulfate tablets, with satisfactory recoveries rates (99.781−106.342%) and relative errors (6.17%). These results indicated that the prepared B,N-CQDs have potential practical application value and broad application prospects.

Cunjin Wang: Writing – original draft. Qian Sun: Conceptualization. Chenxi Li: Investigation. Daibin Tang: Investigation. Huanxian Shi: Investigation. Enzhou Liu: Investigation. Pengqi Guo: Methodology. Weiming Xue: Funding acquisition. Jun Fan: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 11974276), the Project funded by China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2020M683701XB), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (No. 2020JQ-609, 2018JM2037). The authors would also like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TEM and fluorescence lifetime analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.materresbull.2022.111970.
